result:
I’m going to write about the little hinge that the big door of a life swings on—curiosity. Not the capital-letter, TED-talk, explosive kind that supposedly changes industries, but the quiet, almost impolite habit of looking longer than you were invited to. The kind that makes you open the drawer in someone else’s kitchen while you wait for the kettle, not to snoop for secrets but to see how they arrange their teas. One August afternoon when I was eleven, I did exactly that at my aunt’s farmhouse in the Shenandoah Valley. I found a sandwich bag of what I thought was potpourri, buried beneath mismatched mugs. It turned out to be cremated hamster: Little Foot, who had died the previous winter. She—my aunt—hadn’t yet figured out where to scatter him. Standing there, I felt the floor tilt. Death was suddenly a few tablespoons of gray grit that could be mistaken for dried rosemary; a creature who had once zipped up a plastic Habitrail tube was now ballast in a Ziploc. The kettle screamed, my aunt came in, and neither of us mentioned Little Foot. But something had detonated in the harmless cabinet: the recognition that every ordinary object is the tip of an iceberg whose underside can sink you.

That moment should have taught me terror. Instead it taught me inventory. I began to conduct, in every room I entered, an unofficial census: What here is pretending not to be tragic? What here is pretending not to be magic? I became politely nosy, the kid who stayed after class to ask the biology teacher why preserved frogs don’t smell if blood is mostly iron and iron rusts. I asked the janitor why the radiator clicks like it’s dropping marbles. Their answers were never as interesting as the pause they took before answering—an inhale that said no one had audited their expertise in years.

Curiosity is often portrayed as a virtue, but it is also a liability. It makes you late. It gives you detention when you dismantle the school projector “to see if there’s a little picture in there that’s trying to get out.” It makes you read the DSM-IV in the public library and diagnose half your friends, then yourself, then the DSM-IV. It makes you keep the ticket stub from a concert where the opener made you cry even though the stub is just toxic-coated paper that will outlast your memories. Curiosity is metabolic: it devours everything and leaves you woozy, like the sugar-high from a midnight run to Safeway for “research” on whether every store-brand cereal mimics a name-brand one or only the ones shaped like small weapons. (The answer, by the way, is only the weapons: Toasted O’s are Cheerios, but Krave is unavailable in knockoff; apparently no one wants to counterfeit a grenade.)

Yet without that chronic sugar-rush of noticing, I would not have survived the bigger crashes. My mother’s depressions arrived like weather fronts; the house would darken for weeks. While she slept, I wandered the rooms collecting evidence that the world was still turning: dust motes in a shaft of light, the way our refrigerator hummed a perfect B-flat, the neighbor’s cat who could jump a six-foot fence without jangling the bell on its collar. Those observations didn’t fix anything, but they created a lattice, a scaffolding to climb down into another day. Curiosity is not an antidepressant; it’s an architectural element. It builds balconies outside the windowless rooms.

Later, in what grown-ups called real life, I discovered that institutions hate balconies. Corporations want you to stay inside the floorplan, preferably in an open-plan cubicle where “you can be your whole self,” by which they mean the scrap of self that hits KPIs. My first magazine job held daily meetings called “ideation sprints.” We sprinted nowhere; we just brainstormed headlines optimized for algorithms that changed faster than the coffee maker could brew. One day the analytics team declared that curiosity was “a high-value engagement vector.” They installed a whiteboard column labeled “Curiosity Hooks” and pressured us to produce them on schedule. I lasted six months. I left the afternoon I was asked to append a pop-up quiz to an investigation about wildfires: “Which celebrity home do YOU think burned first?” Something in me flashed back to the sandwich bag of hamster ash. I saw the underside of the iceberg again: how curiosity, once monetized, becomes its own arsonist—burning attention for click-throughs, leaving charred cavities where forests of thought used to grow.

So I fled to the opposite economy: teaching in a public high school where bandwidth was so scarce we still used dry-erase boards. Teenagers, it turns out, are curiosity black markets: they traffic in questions that haven’t been declared legal tender. My favorite student wrote poems exclusively about extinct megafauna. Another built a still for a science fair and distilled a single drop of essential oil from forty pinecones, then mailed the oil to her future self with the instruction “Open when the planet is gone.” Nothing they made could be monetized, and therefore—according to every dashboard—nothing they made mattered. Yet every afternoon at 2:57 p.m. the dismissal bell rang like a starter pistol and they sprinted toward the buses clutching things that hadn’t existed that morning: a comic about a dodo who time-travels, a prototype jacket that could turn into a tent, a love letter written on the back of a detention slip. Watching them, I understood that curiosity is not a vector; it is a commons. It can be enclosed, strip-mined, grazed down to bedrock, but it will not file taxes. It re-seeds itself in the cracks.

I kept teaching, kept writing, kept staying late to talk about everything and nothing: whether plants feel loneliness, if the speed of dark is the same as the speed of light, why grief tastes metallic. Then, last spring, my mother died. I flew home and stood again in the farmhouse kitchen. My aunt—still in that kitchen, still with the same mugs—asked if I wanted tea. While the kettle warmed I opened the cabinet. Little Foot was gone. In his place sat a squat glass jar of what looked like coarse gray sand. I didn’t ask. My aunt set two cups on the table. Steam rose between us like a translucent curtain. She said, “Do you ever wonder what happens to the sound of the kettle after we stop hearing it?” We sat until the room itself seemed to vibrate with the echo of water finishing its private scream.

I have no thesis, no three-point plan to bottle that vibration and sell it in the attention economy. I only know that curiosity is the ongoing practice of not turning away. It is the art of remaining in the kitchen after death has removed the familiar shapes, the willingness to ask what sound becomes when it outlives your ears. It is the drawer you open because it might be potpourri and it might be someone’s only remaining ounce, and either way you will hold it in your palms for the length of a kettle’s scream. It is the refusal to pretend that what we call ordinary is safe from tragedy or vacant of miracle.

If you live long enough, every object turns into Little Foot: the birthday card, the ticket stub, the plastic projector wheel. One day the things you collected—those bits of evidence that the world still spins—outnumber your days left to spin with it. That’s when curiosity stops being a hobby and becomes a form of companionship. You pick up the jar, the letter, the child’s comic, and you ask it to tell you again what it saw when you weren’t paying attention. And because you listened to the janitor explain radiator marbles and the teacher explain frog preservatives, because you once dismantled a whole projector and took the detention, the object answers. It says: I was here before you, and I will be here after, but for now we are in the same room. Let’s keep each other company.

That, for roughly a thousand words, is what I wanted to say.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
